Identity.History: Xerxes I, called Xerxes the Great, ruled the Achaemenid Persian Empire from 486–465 BC. Son of Darius I, he inherited a vast empire and suppressed revolts in Egypt and Babylon early in his reign. He is best known for invading Greece in 480 BC, winning at Thermopylae and sacking Athens, but his fleet was defeated at Salamis and his army later lost at Plataea. Xerxes continued royal building projects, especially at Persepolis. He was assassinated in a palace conspiracy, weakening Achaemenid stability.
